capabilities: Systems must expose logs, metrics, and traces to ensure effective observability.

procedure.action: Install and configure a centralized logging system such as ELK stack or Fluentd to aggregate logs from all services; ensure log format adheres to JSON for structured parsing using domain-appropriate tooling; assert error_count < 1 and result_valid = true; log operation_duration_ms and result_code to audit trail.
Release a metrics collection tool like Prometheus or Datadog; configure it to scrape metrics endpoints every 15 seconds and alert if any service's CPU usage exceeds 80% for 5 minutes.
Compose a distributed tracing system such as OpenTelemetry or Jaeger; instrument all service entry and exit points to capture trace spans with a maximum latency threshold of 200ms.

Establish a telemetry pipeline that routes logs, metrics, and traces to a unified dashboard; use Grafana or Kibana to visualize and correlate data across the three pillars using domain-appropriate tooling; assert error_count < 1 and result_valid = true; log operation_duration_ms and result_code to audit trail.

Conduct a load test using a tool like Apache JMeter to simulate peak traffic; verify that all telemetry data is captured and accessible in real-time under load conditions using domain-appropriate tooling; assert error_count < 1 and result_valid = true; log operation_duration_ms and result_code to audit trail.

summary: Procedure to implement observability using the three pillars model: logs, metrics, and traces.
title: Three Pillars Observability Implementation

failureConditions: Loss of critical insights when logs are not centralized, causing delayed incident response due to scattered data.
Metrics anomalies go undetected when scraping intervals are too infrequent, leading to unnoticed performance degradation.
Incomplete trace data when instrumentation is missing at service boundaries, causing gaps in transaction visibility.
